title: Meta-tool architecture for dynamic tool discovery
description: Instead of loading 1000+ tool definitions into context, Composio provides 6 meta tools: SEARCH_TOOLS, GET_TOOL_SCHEMAS, MANAGE_CONNECTIONS, MULTI_EXECUTE_TOOL, REMOTE_WORKBENCH, REMOTE_BASH_TOOL. Agent discovers tools at runtime, loads only what's needed. Solves the context window problem.

title: Native Tools vs MCP tradeoff matrix
description: Native: precise context control, tool call interception (logging/retry/approval), lower token cost. MCP: simpler setup (just URL), client-agnostic, but loads all tools (~55K tokens for 5 servers). Choose native when cost/control matters, MCP when simplicity matters.

title: Session-scoped tool context with session_id correlation
description: Sessions tie together user credentials, available toolkits, and auth config. Meta tool calls share context through session_id — search in one call, execute in next without losing state. User-scoped isolation.

title: Event triggers with webhook + polling dual model
description: Webhook triggers for real-time (GitHub, Slack). Polling triggers for services without webhooks (Gmail, 15min minimum). Structured payloads, user-scoped, connected-account-bound. Enables event-driven agent reactions.

title: Remote workbench as persistent agent execution sandbox
description: Python sandbox where agents write and execute code with Composio tool access. State persists across calls. Handles bulk ops, data transforms, multi-step workflows. Execution layer above basic tool calling.

title: 55K token cost of multi-server MCP setup
description: A 5-server MCP setup consumes ~55K tokens before conversation starts. This is why Composio recommends native tools for cost-conscious applications. Directly relevant to your 3 MCP servers — measure your token overhead.

title: Composio Single Toolkit MCP
description: Wraps any single service as an MCP server. One URL = one toolkit. Simpler than multi-toolkit sessions for focused use cases.

title: llms.txt and llms-full.txt as AI-readable site index
description: Composio publishes llms.txt (index) and llms-full.txt (complete) at site root. Standardized format for LLMs to understand site content. Relevant for JASON-OS documentation strategy.

title: No Claude Code skill integration documented
description: Despite having skills.sh integration, the docs don't show how to use Composio as a Claude Code skill. The skill format and invocation pattern are undocumented.

title: No pricing or rate limit information in docs
description: No discussion of costs, rate limits, or quotas. Critical for evaluating whether Composio is viable for production agent workflows.
